clean_text: I'm about Continuing in Al Wajeez The guitar of Tahara, book of purification. And the sub-chapter regarding the various types of najasa, the various types of filth. Yesterday morning we mentioned the first two, which is urine and feces, human urine and feces. Human urine and feces. Number three and four are al-madhi wal-wadi. Al-madhi wal-wadi. Al-madhi is a discharge that is noted as being clear and it comes out as a result of sexual desire or stimulation. Al-wadi is something that is usually noted as being white, cloudy, and it comes out of the bladder after urinating. These are two things that come from the bladder or two things that come from the from the front private part. And so, al-wadi is clear and it emerges during arousal. Right? Number two is al-wadi with a D, D. D as in dog, wadi. And this comes out of the bladder after urinating. It's not common, it's not normal, it shouldn't come out of the bladder, but usually if there's some kind of infection or you know, someone when they get older in age or something that, al-wadi will start to discharge after the person has finished urinating and all of the urine has come out, after a while this wadi will come out. Both of these are considered to be najis. They are type of najasa. Right? They are type of najasa. So, an-Nawawi rahimahullah shares here that al-madhi, which is something that is a clear discharge that comes when the person becomes aroused, during arousal. This is something that is not particular to men or women. Both men and women have this. So, the evidence that al-madhi is considered a type of najasa is the narration of Ali ibn Abi Talib, which is in Bukhari and Muslim. That he said, "I was someone who had this problem." Meaning in his younger years, he had this issue with al-madhi. The slightest arousal would cause this madhi to discharge and I would be very shy or or I was very bashful to ask the Prophet sallallahu alayhi wasallam regarding the ruling on al-madhi. Now, we take a stop there. We look at Sunan Abi Dawud. Ali ibn Abi Talib mentioned that he really had a hard time with this because he viewed this, I mean, he didn't have the knowledge of the ruling of this. It comes out only as a result of sexual desire. It comes out when one is aroused and it is very closely akin to semen which comes out at at climax and so he would bathe every time this would happen. A bath for najasa for janabah. Radiyallahu anhu. Ijtihad, right? From himself. And he was too bashful to ask the Prophet sallallahu alayhi wasallam cuz he was married to Fatimah radiyallahu anha. So, in Sunan Abi Dawud he says that he fought with this for a long time to the point that in the winter his back was cracking and bleeding from the amount of times he would bathe in the cold, right? Bathing to make salah. And so, he sent al-Miqdad ibn al-Aswad, one of his friends, he sent al-Miqdad ibn al-Aswad to ask the Prophet sallallahu alayhi wasallam on his behalf about the ruling of this. On his behalf, meaning like "Doctor, this is not for me, this is for a friend, right?" On his behalf, not to say Ali sent me, but you know, to make it seem it was his own question. And so, the Prophet sallallahu alayhi wasallam says, "Yaghsilu dhakarahu wa yatawadda." He said he should wash his private parts and make wudu. So, this was a huge relief for Ali radiyallahu anhu. And where do we understand from this sentence? He should wash his private parts and make wudu. From this sentence, where do we get that the conclusion, how do we conclude that it is a type of najasa? Imagine if there was he he had no access to knowledge. He had no access to the Prophet sallallahu alayhi wasallam. He had no access to a person of knowledge. summary: This lecture covers Chapter 1 (Book of Purification) from Al-Wajeez, focusing on the various types of najasa (filth) in Islamic jurisprudence. The instructor discusses three specific discharges that come from the front private part: al-mani (semen), al-madhi (pre-seminal fluid), and al-wadi (post-urination discharge). Each type has distinct characteristics, different rulings regarding purity/impurity, and different requirements for ritual purification (wudu or ghusl). The lesson includes a detailed historical anecdote about Ali ibn Abi Talib seeking guidance from the Prophet Muhammad regarding al-madhi, illustrating the importance of Islamic knowledge in addressing practical matters of daily life.
suggested_title: Al-Wajeez Book of Purification: Types of Najasa - Al-Mani, Al-Madhi, and Al-Wadi
hadith_chapter: Book of Purification (Kitab al-Tahara), Chapter on Types of Najasa (Filth)
catchy_title: Three Discharges: What Islam Actually Says
